---
title: "1. AI Is Your Arm, Not Your Brain"
---

[![Open the lab in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/notebooks/student/nb00_ai_arm_not_brain_student.ipynb){target="_blank"}

> **The research decision.** For every task in your research, you decide whether to
> hand it to an AI tool, and if you do, how you will confirm what it returns before
> you put your name on it. This chapter equips you to draw that line task by task
> and defend where you drew it.

## Why this decision matters

Picture a design review. A **licensed professional engineer (PE)** is an engineer
legally authorized to approve a design, whose signature (their "stamp") makes them
personally answerable for it. Example: the PE who stamps a pedestrian bridge is who
the public trusts if it fails.

When a PE stamps your drawing, they are not saying the software ran without errors.
They are saying, "I checked this, and I will answer for it." That is the posture this
course asks you to adopt on day one. An AI tool can fetch a property, draft a
paragraph, or write working code in seconds, but it cannot stamp your work. You can.
So the question that matters is not "what can the tool produce?" It is "what did I
hand it, and how did I check what came back?"

## The concept

The rule that names the chapter is **AI as your arm, not your brain**: the tool can
reach, fetch, draft, and compute for you, but it does not get to decide what is true
or what you claim. Example: you let it draft a summary of a datasheet, then confirm
every number in that summary yourself.

Two moves put the rule into practice. **Delegation** is handing a well-specified task
to the tool, such as "find three candidate datasheets for this alloy."
**Verification** is an independent check, run by a method outside the model, that the
result is actually true. Example: you open the manufacturer's real datasheet and read
the number yourself instead of trusting what the tool typed.

Verification cannot be skipped because a wrong AI answer looks exactly like a right
one. A **hallucination** is confident, polished output that is simply made up, such as
a citation to a standard that does not exist. Example: a tool reports a fatigue limit
and attributes it to "ASTM E-4471," a number never published. Tone is identical for
real and invented, so tone cannot be your filter. Retrievability is. A source is
**retrievable** when you can open the actual document and confirm it, such as finding
the real standard in the Purdue Libraries and reading the clause.

Some judgments never get delegated. A **never-delegate decision** is a call that stays
yours no matter how good the tool becomes: which question you pursue, what your
evidence licenses, the ethics of the work, and how you state your uncertainty.
Example: a tool may list candidate safety factors, but deciding which one your design
requires is yours to own. The habit that ties this together is short: **Ask, then
Verify, then Document.** Use the tool ambitiously, confirm every fact and number
yourself, then log the tool, the task, and the check in a record that travels with
your work.

## A worked example

You are an honors student sizing the arms of a small quadcopter drone printed from
carbon-fiber-reinforced PLA, a common 3D-printing filament. Each arm is a thin beam,
and if it flexes too much in flight the motors misalign. To size it you need the
material's **flexural modulus**, the number that says how stiff the material is in
bending, where a higher value means the arm bends less under the same load.

Sort the work. You can *delegate* locating candidate datasheets and listing the
properties a beam-bending calculation needs. You *keep* three things: deciding that
flexural modulus (not tensile strength) governs a stiffness problem, choosing the
safety margin, and judging whether a source is authoritative. You ask Gemini for
datasheets and it returns a filament with a flexural modulus of "roughly 4
gigapascals," citing a manufacturer.

Now verify. You open the manufacturer's actual datasheet and read the flexural
modulus line yourself. If it confirms the number, you log a verified input. If it
shows a different value, or cannot be found at all, the number the tool handed you was
never evidence. Either way, the arm rests on a number you retrieved, not one a model
typed.

## The Colab laboratory

This chapter's lab is notebook **nb00, Research in the Age of AI: Your Arm, Your RA,
Not Your Brain**. Open it in Colab with the badge above and work the cells alongside
this chapter. In the lab you run a small seeded simulation showing why how confident a
source *sounds* tells you nothing about whether it is real, then take one topic you
care about, ask an AI for scholarly sources, and verify each by retrieving it
yourself.

## Recommended AI prompts

Commit your own answer first, then delegate. Each prompt hands out a task you can
check, with a verify note naming the failure it defends against.

**Locate candidate sources (you verify each one).**

```text
Act as a materials-research assistant. I need the flexural modulus of
carbon-fiber-reinforced PLA filament. List 3 candidate manufacturer datasheets
or standards, each with the exact document title and where it is published. Only
include documents you are confident exist; mark anything uncertain.
```

After running, verify (counters *confident fabrication*): open each document
yourself and read the number. A datasheet you cannot retrieve is invented until you
find it.

**Turn a decision into a checklist (you audit the list).**

```text
For sizing a printed drone arm as a beam in bending, list every material and
geometric property my deflection calculation needs. For each, say why it belongs
and which single property most governs the result.
```

After running, verify (counters *illusion of completeness*): a long, tidy list can
still omit the property that governs your design. Compare it against a checklist you
wrote first, and confirm the governing property is really the one bending demands.

**Red-team your own choice (you keep the decision).**

```text
Here is my material and safety-factor choice for the drone arm: [paste it]. Act as
a hostile design reviewer. Name every place the choice reaches past my evidence.
Do not rewrite it for me; list the weaknesses.
```

After running, verify (counters *sycophantic agreement*): if every objection is mild
or it calls your choice "well-balanced," push back and ask for the single worst flaw.

::: {.callout-important title="Do not delegate"}
These stay yours, no matter how fluent the tool sounds. Deciding which property
governs your design and what safety margin the part requires. Judging whether a source
is authoritative enough to build on. Setting the claim you will defend and stating its
uncertainty. The tool gathers options and drafts prose; choosing among them, and
answering for the choice, is the researcher's job.
:::

## An AI failure case

You ask for the fatigue limit of your filament and a source. The tool returns a crisp
value and attributes it to "ASTM D-7791, Table 3." It reads like every real citation
you have seen: a standards body, a document number, a table. It is fabricated, or the
number is not on that page.

Here is exactly how you catch it. You do not trust the citation as delivered. You
search the standard in the Purdue Libraries and, if it opens, turn to the named table
and read the line. If the standard does not exist, or has no such value, the confident
citation collapses. Fabricated sources sound just as authoritative as real ones, so
the only reliable filter is opening the document.

## Verification laboratory

The chapter's key result is that polish does not predict truth. Confirm it yourself
with **primary-source reading** (from the [verification guide](https://github.com/davi-moreira/2026F_evidence_driven_research_purdue_HONR464/blob/main/ai_resources/verification_guide.md)):
take a real list of citations an AI returns for a topic you know, search each exact
title, and count how many actually open. The confident-sounding ones are not more
likely to be real than the plain ones. For a number, add **direct calculation**:
recompute the claimed value by hand from the datasheet and check it matches.

## Project transfer

This chapter launches milestone **M0**, your research-and-AI baseline, presented at
Friday's studio. Three moves carry over directly. Sort the tasks in your own project
into safe-to-delegate, delegate-then-verify, and never-delegate, and say why for each.
Run one live citation catch on your topic and record the verdict. Fill your first row
in the AI Research Ledger, logging the task, the tool, your decision, and the
verification method. That ledger row is the habit M0 installs.

## Defend your decision

In a short paragraph, state the delegation line you drew for one task in your own
project: what you handed the tool, what you kept, and the exact check you ran on what
came back. Then name its boundary and your uncertainty. What does your verified
result *not* establish, and where are you most likely to over-trust the tool and stop
checking? A decision with no stated boundary is not yet defended.
